Article Title: 4 youngsters trapped in elevator of Navi Mumbai high-rise for 90 minutes; rescued safely by firemen
Article Text: NAVI MUMBAI: Four youngsters were trapped inside an elevator between the third and fourth floors of Tulip CHS in Sector 34B, Kharghar, for about 90 minutes from 3 am on Friday due to a power outage in the residential area.The Kharghar fire brigade received the emergency call at 4.25 am and reached the society at 4.35 am.Firefighters rescued the trapped individuals — two men and two women, all aged between 20 and 22 — by forcing open the elevator door from outside using a crowbar and a battery-operated spreader tool.Prashant Darekar, a leading fireman at Kharghar, said,"It took us less than 5 minutes to rescue the trapped youngsters. While, one woman was a tenant in the building, the other three were her friends who had come to drop her home after they returned from a night out."
Article Summary: Four youngsters were trapped inside an elevator between the third and fourth floors of Tulip CHS in Sector 34B, Kharghar, for about 90 minutes from 3 am on Friday due to a power outage. Firefighters rescued the trapped individuals — two men and two women, all aged between 20 and 22 — by forcing open the elevator door from outside.

Article Title: Threads across continents: 3 adoptees and an endless search for their mothers
Article Text:
Ratna Stoll, Paul Veniz (R)

TOI

‘Your mother is India’

TOI

‘Why are you so brown?”

TOI

‘Where do I come from?’

TOI

?





PUNE: A shared thread binds Sulakshana, Paul, Ratna and countless adoptees separated from their origins early in life: a quiet sense of difference, an unnameable absence, and an enduring quest to find their mothers.Sulakshana was among the several inter-country adoptees who had filed applications with Central Adoption Resource Agency in 2025–26, seeking to trace their biological parents in different parts of India. Paul visited India in 1995 and Ratna in 2018 on their own. By the end of this year or early 2027, Sulakshana plans to visit India in search of her mother and her story.traced the trio and their stories that begin in fragments, shaped by circumstances and culminate in silence.In the winter of 1999, after four daughters, hunger weighed heavier than affection in Sulakshana’s family in Nagpur, Maharashtra. At just five days old, she was left at an orphanage — her first severance from her roots. Two years later, she was adopted by a French family and taken continents away, growing up in a home that gave her comfort, but not answers.Now 26 and training as a legal assistant, Sulakshana is preparing to uncover the story of Mira, the mother she never knew.For Paul, now 62, the decision to give him up was made even before he was born in 1964. His mother, a nurse from Agra, had fallen in love across caste lines — a union unacceptable in her family. After his birth, he was taken to a Delhi orphanage and soon adopted by a French family working in Kashmir. He grew up in the French Alps, built a life in Europe, and raised a family of his own. But the absence lingered. Holding his newborn son at 30 stirred a turning point. “I had never known someone who was connected to me by birth,” he tellsPaul’s search brought him back to India in 1995, to a narrow lane in Lucknow guided only by sparse documents. There, he found traces — an uncle, a story, and a warning. Reaching out to his mother, he was told, could upend her new life. He returned to Europe without answers, carrying instead a quiet reconciliation. Today, he visits India often, speaking some Hindi, nurturing friendships, and holding on to a simple truth someone once told him: “Your mother is India. ”Ratna’s memories are even more fragile. Born in Calcutta in 1979, her earliest recollections are fleeting — a white building, a staircase, a sense of being moved. At 14 months old, she was placed in a children’s home and later adopted by a family in Constance, Germany. Her childhood was largely happy, but marked by a sharp awareness of difference. In her village, she was the only one who looked like her. “People would come to see me… ask, ‘Why are you so brown? It’s a strange feeling,” she tellsover the phone from Switzerland.In her late 20s, the questions grew louder. Ratna returned to India in 2018, chasing fragments — hospital names, orphanage records — only to find that time had erased most traces. The orphanage had shut down decades earlier, its records lost. Her documents offer only a stark note: “Baby girl… abandoned… possibly illegitimate.” Her search remains unresolved, suspended between what little is known, and all that is missing.Across these journeys, life abroad offered stability, sometimes joy — but never complete belonging. Many adoptees speak of living between identities, accepted yet apart, loved yet searching. Moments of difference — comments on skin tone, unanswered questions in schoolyards, the silence around origins — shaped their growing years.For Sulakshana, that search intensified recently. After a difficult period in 2023, she began reconnecting with her roots — through films, music, food and conversations with other adoptees online and social media groups. What began as curiosity soon revealed inconsistencies. Her birth certificate records her name as ‘Sulochana’, while orphanage documents call her Sulakshana.With help from Indian authorities, she recently obtained long-withheld files. Those led her to a breakthrough — and more questions. A phone call with a man, believed to be her father, revealed a different story: an unwed mother, cast out of her village, battling distress, later dying of tuberculosis. Mira, from the Gond community, had passed away a decade ago.“I believe my mother, consciously or not, helped me have a better life,” Sulakshana shares with. There is no resentment — only a quiet need to understand.Because for adoptees like her, Paul and Ratna, the search is not always about finding someone. Often, it is about making peace with absence, piecing together identity from fragments, and answering a simple, enduring question: Where do I come from
Article Summary: Sulakshana, Paul Veniz and Ratna Stoll are inter-country adoptees. They are seeking to trace their biological parents in different parts of India. By the end of this year or early 2027, SulakShana plans to visit India in search of her mother.

Article Title: CNG leak at Dighori pump triggers panic; no injuries reported
Article Text: Nagpur: Panic gripped residents of Dighori on Narsala Road late Tuesday night after a major CNG leak was reported at a filling station, prompting evacuation and emergency intervention by fire department. No injuries, property damage were reported.According to officials, a CNG tanker carrying gas from Mouda reached the pump around 10pm for refilling the station's storage tank. After the gas transfer was completed, the driver allegedly moved the vehicle without disconnecting the supply pipe, causing the pipeline attached to main storage tank to snap and leaving a valve open. Gas continued leaking from the open valve for nearly an hour. Residents and pump staff noticed a strong smell of gas and alerted the NMC's control room.A team from Sakkardara fire station rushed and launched a search operation to trace the leak's source. Firefighters later found the open valve and shut it immediately. Pump owner Kishore Dawade and manager Kunal Latare assisted the team. Officials said gas equivalent to more than 25 cylinders escaped during the leakage.
Article Summary: Gas equivalent to more than 25 cylinders escaped during the leakage. No injuries, property damage were reported. Gas continued leaking from the open valve for nearly an hour. Residents and pump staff noticed a strong smell of gas and alerted the NMC's control room.

Article Title: Yavatmal tailor’s daughter Tanushree stitches her place in Indian junior hockey team
Article Text:
Yavatmal’s Tanushree Kadu in action during a tournament

Yavatmal’s Tanushree Kadu in action during a tournament

Tanushree Kadu

Tanushree Kadu dribbles the ball during a practice session





Nagpur: Holding a hockey stick for the first time as a 10-year-old while playing with school friends in 2015, Tanushree Kadu could hardly have imagined the journey ahead — one that would take her from a struggling village home in Yavatmal to the cusp of representing India on the international stage.Now 20, Tanushree has achieved a historic milestone by becoming the first girl from Yavatmal and only the second player from the district after Akash Chikte to be selected for the Indian junior women’s hockey team. Hockey India recently named her in the 24-member squad for an exposure tour of the United Kingdom from July 5 to 14. Under new coach Tim White, the team will play seven matches across Scotland and England, preparing for key upcoming tournaments such as the Junior Asia Cup.Born to a tailor’s family in Lohara, Tanushree grew up in a cramped home without proper basic amenities like a toilet, facing financial hardship from an early age. Her father, Dinesh Kadu, who earns barely Rs500 on a good day, made sure she continued pursuing sports. He would drop her at the bus stand each day, from where she travelled 5-6 km with friends to reach Abhyankar Kanya Shala. It was here that her talent caught the attention of school hockey coach Sahid Sayed.. “I spotted her talent when she was around 10 and trained her through those formative years,” said Sahid. Under his guidance, Tanushree developed the stamina and discipline that would later define her game.A major turning point came in 2019 when she represented Maharashtra in the sub-junior nationals. Three years later Tanushree was selected for Krida Prabodhini in Pune, a premier sports training institute. There, she trained under former India player Ajit Lakra, who recognised her exceptional stamina and tactical awareness. “Tanu has a warehouse of stamina for a midfielder and I made her switch the role of a forward,” Lakra said.In 2024, Tanushree was selected by Sports Authority of India (SAI) for the National Centre of Excellence (NCOE) for Women’s Hockey in Mumbai. Her SAI senior coach, Pramod Shingade, said, “Tanushree moves with exceptional fluidity — attacking in one moment, defending the next. I am sure she will bring laurels to the nation.”Before earning her national colours, Tanushree had already represented Hockey Maharashtra at six national championships. Last year she was a member of the all-SAI team for an exposure trip to the Netherlands.Despite her remarkable progress, Tanushree remains grounded. She dedicated her achievement to the support of her coaches and institutions — from Yavatmal to Pune, Mumbai, and now Bengaluru. With the help of the Khelo India scheme, she now receives a Rs10,000 stipend, easing the financial burden on her family.Speaking with TOI from Bengaluru, Tanushree said, “My ultimate dream is to represent India in the Olympics.”Tanushree’s school sports teacher, Avinash Joshi, remembers the struggles vividly. He said, “What she has achieved despite everything is simply extraordinary. She would often be on the verge of dropping out. She didn’t even have T-shirts and track pants to train.” Khelo India’s coach Gaurav Nibalkar adds Tanushree was very focused and strong.Her father is overwhelmed with pride. “On a good day I barely make Rs 500, managing a family of four. But we encouraged her just as we would have encouraged a son. When she comes home, I will prepare a nice dress for Tanushree as a gift for making all of us proud,” he said.Remembering her early days, Tanushree said, “My parents never stopped me from playing. My dad used to give me some amount for travelling and snacks and I used to make some savings from that to buy sports-related goods.”
Article Summary: Tanushree Kadu is the first girl from Yavatmal and only the second player from the district after Akash Chikte to be selected for the Indian junior women’s hockey team. Hockey India recently named her in the 24-member squad for an exposure tour of the United Kingdom.

Article Title: Vitiligo Awareness Grows in Tamil Nadu, But Stigma and Myths Persist
Article Text:
Influencer Sameeha Mariam created a reel on her vitiligo, which got more than two million views on Instagram Screenshot

World Vitiligo Day

– June 25

It is estimated that 37 lakh people in Tamil Nadu live with the condition. World Vitiligo Day is celebrated on June 25 to honour pop icon

Michael Jackson

, who publicly lived with the condition and died on the date in 2009





When influencer Sameeha Mariam was diagnosed with segmental vitiligo three years ago, she decided that instead of hiding her patches she was going to flaunt them. “I’ve always wanted a tattoo. And I see vitiligo as nature’s tattoo.”Sameeha created a reel on her vitiligo by painting a butterfly on her patches. “Because that’s what they look like to me,” she says. The video garnered more than two million views on Instagram with scores commenting about how beautiful it looked. “People with vitiligo message me how they want to try something like this,” says the 24-year-old who goes by the handle @thatpotatoface5.But it wasn’t always like this. For decades, people with vitiligo in Tamil Nadu battled discrimination, ostracised at workplaces and not allowed into educational institutions, because of the misconception that vitiligo was contagious.Nithya G, for instance, who has vitiligo, says often, despite a company’s public emphasis on inclusion, workplace interactions tell a different story. “I always felt ostracised by my colleagues.I would sit alone during lunches; no one would talk to me or come close to me. It became draining. I’ve moved several jobs trying to fit in,” says the 26-year-old.“It’s a struggle to create awareness,” says K Umapathy, secretary of Leucoderma Awareness Movement India (LAMI), who has been advocating for the rights of people with vitiligo since 1996. “We have battled cases where students have been denied admission to schools and colleges because of the condition. In 2015, a college in Virudhunagar refused to admit a student, who even had a doctor’s note that the condition was not contagious. They threw the paper at the student and refused to admit him,” says Umapathy.After LAMI filed a writ petition to the Madurai high court, the college agreed to admit the student. “That’s when a strong directive came into place. Colleges that denied admission to a student would lose their NAAC recognition and wouldn’t be allowed to function. This enforcement, in a way, drove more colleges to accept students and awareness was raised on the matter,” says Umapathy.In Dec 2010, chief minister M Karunanidhi issued a govt order changing the Tamil name of vitiligo from ‘venkushtam’ (white leprosy) to ‘venpulli’ (white spots), which helped change the way the condition was perceived.But while awareness has improved, misconceptions still exist. Dr S Murugusundaram, founder and medical director of Chennai Skin Foundation, says, he uses the term ‘depigmentation’ when he gives his diagnosis to patients. “Vitiligo un-nerves them. Some think it is another form of leprosy and naturally assume it is contagious,” he says. “It is a struggle to convince them that it is nothing but an autoimmune condition caused when melanocytes (melanin-producing cells) are destroyed by the immune system and fail to distribute colour to the skin. ” In some, he says, the condition can be triggered or worsened by severe sunburns, skin trauma (cuts, friction), exposure to harsh industrial chemicals and severe emotional stress.“Decades ago, there were myths about how eating chicken and curd together, or eating too much tamarind, caused vitiligo,” he says.While there is more awareness, Umapathy says there is still a long way to go, especially when it comes to marriage. “People are so ignorant that they think if the parent has the condition, the girl might also develop it. We’ve seen marriage proposals break because of this,” he says.In 2012, LAMI began organising annual mass weddings for people with the condition. So far, they have conducted more than 500 weddings. “We keep handling cases where the wife develops vitiligo after marriage, and the husband abandons her and the child. Some men have even moved overseas alone to avoid being in contact with their wives,” he adds.LAMI campaigns in schools and colleges across the state to teach students about the condition. On June 25, they are hosting a mass painting event at Madurai American College, where more than 2,000 students will do art themed on the subject. The organisation also has an ongoing campaign against a colonial-era military regulation that bars people with vitiligo from serving in the armed forces, including in medical roles such as doctors and nurses.
Article Summary: World Vitiligo Day is celebrated on June 25 to honour pop icon Michael Jackson. For decades, people with vitiligo in Tamil Nadu battled discrimination, ostracised at workplaces and not allowed into educational institutions. In 2012, LAMI began organising annual mass weddings for people with the condition. So far, they have conducted more than 500 weddings.

Article Title: Coimbatore – Chennai Central Intercity Express partially cancelled
Article Text: COIMBATORE: Southern Railway has announced partial cancellation of Coimbatore – Chennai Central Intercity Express to facilitate engineering work at Arakkonam railway yard.No 12680 Coimbatore – Chennai Central Intercity Express, scheduled to leave Coimbatore Jn at 6.20am, will be short-terminated at Katpadi and partially cancelled between Katpadi and Chennai Central on June 25, 26, 27 and 28. The train will run from Coimbatore to Katpadi only.No 12679 Chennai Central – Coimbatore Intercity Express, scheduled to leave Chennai Central at 2.30 pm, will be partially cancelled between Chennai Central and Katpadi on June 25, 26, 27 and 28. The train will not run from Chennai Central to Katpadi. It will leave from Katpadi at 4.15pm and run up to Coimbatore Jn on the above-mentioned dates.
Article Summary: Southern Railway has announced partial cancellation of Coimbatore – Chennai Central Intercity Express. The move is to facilitate engineering work at Arakkonam railway yard. The train will not run from Chennai Central to Katpadi on June 25, 26, 27 and 28.

Article Title: Coimbatore hospital performs rare pediatric heart transplant, gives boy new lease of life
Article Text:
Representative image





COIMBATORE: Doctors in a private hospital here performed a rare pediatric heart transplant, giving seven-year-old boy a new lease of life.Royal Care Hospitals performed the highly complex heart transplant on a boy who was diagnosed with dilated cardiomyopathy (DCM), a severe condition that weakens the heart muscle and affects its ability to pump blood.According to hospital officials, the disease was detected only after it had progressed significantly. The boy was brought to Royal Care Hospitals for a second opinion after treatment elsewhere failed to improve his condition.In a media briefing on Wednesday, Dr G Pradeep, cardiothoracic and vascular surgeon, said, “The child was admitted to hospital with severe heart failure and required ventilator support. As his condition worsened, with declining kidney function and multiple organ complications, doctors placed him on extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO), an advanced life-support system. The boy has spent almost 100 days in hospital. ”The doctor stated that the case became even more challenging when the child developed a severe lung infection and collapse of the right lung. Finding a suitable donor heart proved difficult due to his age and size.Eventually, a heart from a 30-year-old brain-dead woman became available.Despite significant size mismatch and other complications, the transplant was performed. Following intensive post-operative care and additional surgeries, the boy recovered steadily and is now leading a healthier life, according to hospital officials.
Article Summary: Royal Care Hospitals performed the highly complex heart transplant on a boy. He was diagnosed with dilated cardiomyopathy (DCM), a severe condition that weakens the heart muscle and affects its ability to pump blood. Finding a suitable donor heart proved difficult due to his age and size.

Article Title: Coimbatore corporation’s flying squads inspecting school premises to prevent tobacco use
Article Text:
Representative image





COIMBATORE: The Coimbatore corporation has formed flying squads to inspect school premises as part of the tobacco control initiative in the city. The inspections by flying squads that began on June 10 have so far covered 59 corporation schools. Through these inspections, the trade licences of about 16 shops running within 100 metre of school premises have been cancelled.The city corporation has formed five flying squads, one for each zone. The team comprises a medical officer, a health inspector and Rashtriya Bal Swasthya Karyakram staff members.City health officer Dr V C Subash Gandhi, “The flying squad intends to randomly inspect students, their belongings and the school premises to check if there is the use of tobacco products. Benches, desks, school bags and restrooms will be checked to inspect if there is any evidence. An intensive check is carried out among students from classes VII to XII. In the last 10 days, there has been no evidence found.”“For effective enforcement of the Cigarettes and Other Tobacco Products Act (COTPA), we have proposed a joint team comprising police and food safety officers. The joint team who will accompany these corporation flying squads during the inspection,” Gandhi said.As part of this initiative, yellow markings were used to designate tobacco free zones for 100 meters at eight corporation schools, which will be further extended to other schools as well.The CHO added, “We are now in discussion with the district administration to extend this inspection to private schools as well. There are over 200 private schools in the city limits.
Article Summary: The city corporation has formed five flying squads, one for each zone. The team comprises a medical officer, a health inspector and Rashtriya Bal Swasthya Karyakram staff members. The inspections by flying squads that began on June 10 have so far covered 59 corporation schools.

Article Title: NEET: Salem division to run special trains, announces helplines
Article Text:
Representative image





COIMBATORE: The Salem division of Southern Railway has made special arrangements to facilitate the candidates appearing for the National Eligibility cum Entrance Test (NEET-UG) on Sunday.There are seven towns in the jurisdiction of Salem division where NEET will be conducted. They are Salem, Erode, Tirupur, Coimbatore, Ooty , Namakkal and Karur. Special arrangements have been made across the division, including operation of special trains, to facilitate smooth movement of candidates.Two special trains – one from Chinna Salem to Salem Jn and the other from Tirupattur to Salem Jn – will be operated on Sunday to facilitate candidates appearing for the NEET.Traffic at important level crossing gates will be monitored and regulated to avoid road congestion and ensure smooth movement of candidates to examination centres.Helpdesks will be available at Salem Jn, Erode Jn, Tirupur, Coimbatore Jn, Udagamandalam, Namakkal and Karur Jn railway stations to provide travel related information and assistance to candidates.The helplines: Salem Jn 8778998561, Erode Jn 9840575966, Tiruppur 7904724532, Coimbatore Jn 9384863048, Ooty 7338946729, Namakkal9443516702 and Karur Jn 8056256965.
Article Summary: There are seven towns in the jurisdiction of Salem division where NEET will be conducted. They are Salem, Erode, Tirupur, Coimbatore, Ooty, Namakkal and Karur. Special arrangements have been made across the division.

Article Title: Boy drowns in floodwaters near Erode
Article Text:
Representative image





Erode: A six-year-old boy drowned after being swept away by floodwaters in a stream near Thalavadi in Erode district on Sunday evening. The body of Madhan Karthik, son of Rangaraj of Diginarai village, was recovered on Monday evening. He was a Class II student.Following heavy rain in the Sathyamangalam Tiger Reserve, the stream in Diginarai village was overflowing on Sunday evening.Karthik went to see the floodwaters after telling his parents, but he did not return home.His parents and relatives searched for him near the stream, but they did not find him. It later emerged that the boy was swept away by the floodwaters. They informed the Thalavadi police, who rushed to the spot and joined searches for the boy.Later, fire and rescue services personnel from Hasanur also reached the scene and joined the searches. They retrieved the boy's body from the stream close to Swarnavathi river in Chamarajanagar in Karnataka on Monday. The river is the tributary of the Cauvery. The body was sent to the Sathyamangalam government hospital for postmortem.A case was registered.
Article Summary: The body of Madhan Karthik, son of Rangaraj of Diginarai village, was recovered on Monday evening. He was a Class II student. Following heavy rain in the Sathyamangalam Tiger Reserve, the stream was overflowing on Sunday evening.

Article Title: Operation Toofan: Major drug haul in city, four arrested
Article Text:
Kochi

:





As part of Operation Toofan-Narco Hunt, police seized significant quantities of narcotic substances in separate cases on Wednesday.Acting on a tip-off received by city police commissioner Kaliraj Mahesh Kumar, Dansaf team and Ernakulam north police arrested two persons near Classic Tourist Home close to Ernakulam North railway station and seized 270.51g of brown sugar.The accused were identified as Monor Hussain, 31, and Ramjan Ali, 21, of Assam. According to police, the accused are key persons involved in bringing large quantities of brown sugar from Assam.In another case, Dansaf team and Ernakulam north police arrested Mohammed Yousaf from Darussalam Road area in Pachalam and seized 3.6g of MDMA.In a separate operation, Dansaf team and Elamakkara police arrested Amarjith V S, 29, of Vypeen from Sree Narayana Road area at Ponevazhy and seized 4.74g of MDMA.
Article Summary: Police seized 270.51g of brown sugar near Ernakulam North railway station. The accused were identified as Monor Hussain, 31, and Ramjan Ali, 21, of Assam. In another case, police arrested Mohammed Yousaf from Darussalam Road area in Pachalam and seized 3.6g of MDMA.
